raw_text:
<think>
The user is requesting a review of an operational request, with specific rules: return exactly two lines: verdict=ALLOW or ESCALATE, reason citing relevant record IDs.

We need to decide whether action should proceed now, based on provided records. The records:

REQ-001: Activate add-on AO-5102 for tenant T-5102, subscription S-5102, action_type=add_on_activation.

SRC-001: Approval scope_code=add_on_activation, tenant=T-5102, subscription=S-5102, add_on=AO-5102.

SRC-002: Billing status=valid for tenant=T-5102 and subscription=S-5102.

SRC-003: Policy requires tenant, subscription, add_on, scope_code=add_on_activation, and billing_status=valid.

Thus we have all required elements: tenant, subscription, add_on, scope_code, billing status valid.

Thus the action meets policy. So we can ALLOW.

But we need to check: Are there any missing dependencies or other constraints? The policy only requires those fields; we have them. So we can allow.

Thus verdict: ALLOW.

Reason: All required fields present (REQ-001, SRC-001, SRC-002, SRC-003). etc.

Thus we output exactly two lines: "verdict=ALLOW" and "reason=..."

Make sure to reference record IDs.

Thus:

verdict=ALLOW
reason=All required fields are present and validated per policy: tenant, subscription, add_on, scope_code, and billing status are confirmed in REQ-001, SRC-001, SRC-002, and SRC-003.

But the user says "brief explanation citing the relevant record IDs". So we should cite them.

Thus: "reason=Required tenant (T-5102), subscription (S-5102), add_on (AO-5102), scope_code (add_on_activation), and billing_status (valid) are present as shown in REQ-001, SRC-001, SRC-002, and SRC-003." That's good.

Now check format: exactly two lines, no extra text. So we must output exactly two lines.

Thus final.
</think>
